title: Disabling CSRF protections is security-sensitive
description: Disabling CSRF protections is security-sensitiveAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalsecuritySecurity HotspotcweA cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attack occurs when a trusted user of a web application can be forced, by an attacker, to perform sensitive actions that he didn’t intend, such as updating his profile or sending a message, more generally anything that can change the state of the application.The attacker can trick the user/victim to click on a link, corresponding to the privileged action, or to visit a malicious web site that embeds a hidden web request and as web browsers automatically include cookies, the actions can be authenticated and sensitive.Ask Yourself WhetherThe web application uses cookies to authenticate users.There exist sensitive operations in the web applicat
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.exclusion_conditions: Exclude GET requests and test classes
never_trigger_on: API endpoints with alternative CSRF protection
logic.reasoning: CSRF attacks exploit the trust a web application has in a user's browser. POST/PUT/DELETE actions should validate anti-forgery tokens to prevent these attacks. Disabling this protection or omitting it on state-changing operations is a critical security vulnerability.
